title: Culture and Anarchy
description: Culture and Anarchy — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Culture and Anarchy" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: "Chapter I: Sweetness and Light"
memes.description: "On culture as the pursuit of perfection — sweetness and light against narrow Puritanism."

memes.label: "Chapter II: Doing as One Likes"
memes.description: "On the dangers of English individualism and the fetish of personal liberty."

memes.label: "Chapter III: Barbarians, Philistines, Populace"
memes.description: "Arnold's famous tripartite classification of English society and the role of culture in transforming each class."

memes.label: "Chapter IV: Hebraism and Hellenism"
memes.description: "On the two competing forces in Western civilization — Hebraism (moral duty) and Hellenism (intellectual clarity)."

memes.label: "Chapter Notes"
memes.description: "Editorial notes on notation, pagination, and chapter titles."

memes.label: "Chapter V: Porro Unum est Necessarium"
memes.description: "On the one thing needful — the balance of Hebraism and Hellenism in pursuing perfection."

memes.label: "Chapter VI: Our Liberal Practitioners"
memes.description: "A critique of contemporary Liberal reform movements and the practical application of culture to politics."

memes.label: "Front Matter"
memes.description: "Gutenberg eBook header with title, author, and publication metadata."

memes.label: "Project Gutenberg License"
memes.description: "The full Project Gutenberg™ License terms."

memes.label: "Preamble"
memes.description: "Opening preamble on culture and its critics, responding to Mr. Bright and Mr. Frederic Harrison."

memes.label: "Preface (1869 First Edition)"
memes.description: "Arnold's original preface addressing the Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge, Bishop Wilson, and the purpose of culture."

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents — Culture and Anarchy by Matthew Arnold (1869)'

memes.label: Académie Française
memes.description: The French Academy, used by Arnold as a counter-example of cultural authority that England lacks but should not blindly imitate

memes.label: America
memes.description: The United States, which Arnold treats throughout Culture and Anarchy as the great cautionary example of democracy without adequate authority — a nation whose religious vitality and political energy cannot compensate for its lack of 'the best self' and its worship of the 'average man'

memes.label: Anarchy
memes.description: The social condition Arnold's culture opposes — not political anarchism but the general dissolution of order, authority, and right reason that results when "doing as one likes" becomes the governing principle

memes.label: The Anglican Establishment
memes.description: The institutional complex of the Church of England in its legally privileged, socially embedded form — discussed by Arnold as both the target of Nonconformist antipathy and a potential vehicle for national culture if reformed along "latitudinarian" lines.

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher whose ethical concept of virtue as a mean and whose observation about the few who pursue "sweetness and light" Arnold deploys as rhetorical touchstones in Culture and Anarchy.

memes.label: Authority
memes.description: Arnold's concept of the legitimate power that embodies right reason and the collective best self — contrasted with both the mechanical authority of class privilege and the anarchy of doing as one likes

memes.label: Benjamin Franklin
memes.description: American statesman and philosopher, cited in Culture and Anarchy as a representative of utilitarian "machinery" thinking that mistakes means for ends.

memes.label: Jeremy Bentham
memes.description: English philosopher, jurist, and social reformer (1748–1832), founder of modern utilitarianism, whom Arnold critiques as representing the narrowness of systematic thinking in Culture and Anarchy.

memes.label: Birmingham
memes.description: The industrial city in the English Midlands that Arnold associates with John Bright's political platform and the assertive Nonconformist liberalism he critiques — a metonym for the provincial, commerce-driven culture of the Philistine class.

memes.label: Bishop Wilson
memes.description: Thomas Wilson (1663–1755), Anglican bishop of Sodor and Man, whose Maxims of Piety and Christianity provided Arnold with the key phrase 'to make reason and the will of God prevail' — the recurring motto of Arnoldian culture

memes.label: Book of Leviticus
memes.description: The third book of the Torah and the Old Testament, referred to in Culture and Anarchy in the context of Mosaic law, its interpretation by English Puritanism, and Arnold's critique of Hebraic legalism.

memes.label: The British Banner
memes.description: A Nonconformist newspaper that attacked Arnold's cultural lectures, cited by him in Chapter III as an example of the religious press's inability to comprehend culture as anything other than a threat to Protestant orthodoxy.

memes.label: British Figures
memes.description: All person pages with British nationality in the Culture and Anarchy wiki — the politicians, clergy, writers, and public intellectuals who populate Arnold's critique of Victorian England.

memes.label: Charles Augustin Sainte-Beuve
memes.description: Celebrated French literary critic whose method Arnold discusses in the opening chapter of Culture and Anarchy as an exemplar of intellectual curiosity.

memes.label: Charles Bradlaugh
memes.description: English secularist, republican, and political activist whom Arnold repeatedly invokes as the "excess" pole of working-class political energy, standing opposite the "virtuous mean" of George Odger.

memes.label: Charles Buxton
memes.description: British Liberal MP and writer, described by Arnold as a "philosophical politician" who swims with the current of popular Nonconformist opinion.

memes.label: Charles Spurgeon
memes.description: Influential Baptist preacher whose Metropolitan Tabernacle sermons and Nonconformist "voluntaryism" Arnold critiques as exemplifying the Hebraic narrowing of religion to a mechanical principle of "do as you like" in ecclesiastical affairs.

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The established national church of England, which Arnold defends in principle while critiquing its complacency — a key institution in his argument about authority and the State

memes.label: Commercial Travellers
memes.description: A trade association representing travelling salesmen, cited by Arnold alongside the Licensed Victuallers as an example of the self-interested, class-bound character of Philistine educational and social initiatives.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept pages in the Culture and Anarchy wiki, sorted alphabetically with quality tier and citation count.

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The internal moral faculty that Hebraism cultivates — the voice that warns against sin and urges right conduct; essential but, Arnold argues, insufficient without the balancing influence of Hellenic intelligence

memes.label: Culture
memes.description: The central concept of Arnold's social philosophy — the disinterested pursuit of human perfection through knowing the best that has been thought and said, combining intellectual, moral, and social dimensions

memes.label: Culture and Anarchy
memes.description: The 1869 book by Matthew Arnold that is the subject of this wiki — a landmark work of Victorian social and cultural criticism arguing for the transformative role of culture in countering the anarchy of unchecked individualism.
aliases: Culture and Anarchy: An Essay in Political and Social Criticism

relations.label: Chapter II: Doing as One Likes
Chapter V: Porro Unum est Necessarium

memes.label: The Daily News
memes.description: A London daily newspaper founded by Charles Dickens in 1846, discussed by Arnold in Chapter III as a voice of Liberal and Nonconformist opinion that misunderstands and dismisses his cultural criticism.

memes.label: The Daily Telegraph
memes.description: A popular London daily newspaper founded in 1855, serving as Arnold's principal example of Philistine journalism and the "young lions" of the popular press who substitute noise and sensation for reason and culture.

memes.label: Sir Daniel Gooch
memes.description: English railway engineer, industrialist, and politician (1816–1889), whose mother's maxim about aspiring to be "manager of that concern" Arnold presents as the quintessential expression of British industrial Philistinism.

memes.label: Deborah Butler
memes.description: American religious figure and spiritualist, named by Arnold among the obscure sectarian leaders whose exaggerated treatment by popular writers illustrates the democratic flattening of intellectual authority.

memes.label: Doing as One Likes
memes.description: Arnold's phrase for the Englishman's characteristic notion of freedom — the right to do whatever one pleases, without regard for reason, authority, or the common good
aliases: liberty of doing as one likes

memes.label: East of London
memes.description: The working-class districts of London's East End, which Matthew Arnold visited and reflected upon in Culture and Anarchy as a site of social observation and moral challenge.

memes.label: Edmond Beales
memes.description: Middle-class radical leader and president of the Reform League, used by Arnold as an example of the Philistine activist sacrificed to political machinery.

memes.label: Rev. Edward White
memes.description: English Nonconformist minister and writer against Church Establishments, whom Arnold engages with critically in the Preface to Culture and Anarchy.

memes.label: England
memes.description: The nation whose character and institutions form the primary object of Arnold's social critique — simultaneously the home of 'doing as one likes' and the potential site of cultural renewal

memes.label: The Established Church
memes.description: The Church of England in its character as a legally established national church, discussed by Arnold as both an institution of cultural value and a target of Nonconformist antipathy — embodying the tension between the historic ideal of a comprehensive national church and the reality of religious Dissent.

memes.label: Establishment
memes.description: Arnold's term for the entrenched institutional and cultural order — the Church of England, the aristocracy, the class system — whose authority he critiques as mechanical but whose principle he defends as necessary against anarchy

memes.label: France
memes.description: The continental nation that Arnold repeatedly uses as a point of comparison with England — associated with the Académie Française as a model of centralized cultural authority, and with the French Revolution as a cautionary example of reform without the proper balance of Hebraism and Hellenism.

memes.label: Frederick Robertson
memes.description: English Anglican preacher, praised in Culture and Anarchy for a sermon on Greece that Arnold considers rhetorically powerful but intellectually problematic.

memes.label: George Herbert
memes.description: English metaphysical poet and Anglican priest (1593–1633), quoted by Arnold in Culture and Anarchy for his devotional verse

memes.label: George Odger
memes.description: Working-class trade unionist and radical leader whom Arnold selects as the "virtuous mean" of the English working class — honest and sympathetic but lacking in "light."

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: The nation that Arnold holds up throughout Culture and Anarchy as the modern exemplar of Hellenism — a country whose intellectual seriousness, philosophical depth, and balanced cultivation of human powers stand in sharp contrast to English Philistinism and provide a model for cultural renewal

memes.label: Gotthold Ephraim Lessing
memes.description: Gotthold Ephraim Lessing (1729–1781), German dramatist, critic, and philosopher of the Enlightenment, whom Matthew Arnold invokes alongside Herder as an exemplar of the "great men of culture" who humanised knowledge and diffused sweetness and light across German society at the end of the eighteenth century.

memes.label: Ancient Greece
memes.description: The classical civilization that Arnold holds up as the source of the Hellenic ideal — the standard of intellectual and aesthetic cultivation against which he measures modern English life and finds it wanting.

memes.label: Hebraism
memes.description: Arnold's term for the moral and religious impulse that prioritizes conduct, obedience, and the strict governance of desire — one of the two great spiritual forces shaping Western civilization

memes.label: Heinrich Heine
memes.description: Heinrich Heine (1797–1856), German poet, journalist, and critic, the greatest German lyric poet after Goethe — Arnold knew his work intimately and lectured on him, and his single allusion in Culture and Anarchy invokes Heine's mockery of the "Philistine" to reinforce Arnold's own anatomy of English middle-class narrowness.

memes.label: Hellenism
memes.description: One of the two great spiritual forces in Arnold's framework — the impulse to see things as they really are, emphasizing intellectual clarity, beauty, and the harmonious development of all human faculties

memes.label: Henry Thomas Buckle
memes.description: English historian and author of History of Civilisation, whom Arnold critiques as a representative of scientific and rationalist fanaticism.

memes.label: Henry Sidgwick
memes.description: Cambridge philosopher and contemporary critic of Arnold who argued that "fire and strength" from religion are needed more than "sweetness and light" from culture.

memes.label: Hepworth Dixon
memes.description: English historian and travel writer whose sensational accounts of Mormonism and American religious sects supply Arnold with examples of the British public's inability to distinguish true authority from mere popularity.

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: The elected lower house of the British Parliament, whose Reform debates Arnold attended and whose proceedings become, in Culture and Anarchy, a symbol of political machinery operating without the guidance of right reason or 'the best self'

memes.label: Hyde Park
memes.description: London's principal royal park, site of the 1866 Reform League demonstrations and a focal point for Victorian debates about public order and political rights.

memes.label: Hyde Park Riots 1866
memes.description: The July 1866 disturbances in Hyde Park, when a Reform League demonstration escalated into the destruction of park railings — Arnold's prime example of 'doing as one likes' dissolving into anarchy

memes.label: Ireland
memes.description: The neighboring island whose political and religious conflicts — particularly the question of the Irish Church establishment — Arnold uses as a lens through which to examine the character of English reform and the limitations of Nonconformist political reasoning.

memes.label: Church of Ireland
memes.description: The established Protestant church in Ireland, whose disestablishment Arnold discusses as a case study of Nonconformist-driven reform — an institution that, though legally privileged, had lost any genuine spiritual connection to the majority of the Irish people.

memes.label: Disestablishment of the Irish Church
memes.description: The 1869 legislative process to sever the Church of Ireland from state establishment, used by Arnold as a case study in how Nonconformist antipathy drives reform without adequate Hellenic examination.

memes.label: Jacob Bright
memes.description: British Liberal MP, brother of John Bright, whom Arnold presents as representative of the middle-class Liberal spirit.

memes.label: Joseph Smith
memes.description: Founder of Mormonism, referenced in Culture and Anarchy as an example of religious enthusiasm examined with sympathetic curiosity.

memes.label: Johann Gottfried Herder
memes.description: Johann Gottfried Herder (1744–1803), German philosopher, theologian, and literary critic, a central figure of the Sturm und Drang and Weimar Classicism, whom Arnold invokes alongside Lessing as one of the "great men of culture" who humanised knowledge and broadened the basis of German intellectual life.

memes.label: John Bright
memes.description: John Bright (1811–1889), radical Liberal statesman and orator, whom Arnold critiques as an exemplar of the middle-class Philistine — vigorous, sincere, but culturally narrow and hostile to the claims of 'sweetness and light'

memes.label: John Calvin
memes.description: John Calvin (1509–1564), French theologian and principal figure of the Reformed tradition, whom Arnold invokes in the Preface to Culture and Anarchy in the context of Church Establishment debates — the "friends of Calvin's discipline" and the institutional settlement of the Reformation.

memes.label: John Henry Newman
memes.description: English theologian and leader of the Oxford Movement, whose conflict with mid-Victorian liberalism Arnold invokes as a defining illustration of the liberal worldview.

memes.label: John Humphrey Noyes
memes.description: American utopian socialist and founder of the Oneida Community, referenced by Arnold as an example of Nonconformist provincialism.

memes.label: John Arthur Roebuck
memes.description: Liberal MP whose argument that every Englishman "may say what he likes" is cited by Arnold as the quintessential expression of faith in machinery over inward perfection.

memes.label: John Stuart Mill
memes.description: John Stuart Mill (1806–1873), English philosopher, political economist, and Member of Parliament, the pre-eminent liberal thinker of the Victorian age — Arnold mentions him once in Culture and Anarchy, grouping him with Buckle as a rationalist system-builder whose influence the men of culture must resist.

memes.label: John Wesley
memes.description: English cleric, theologian, and founder of Methodism, whom Arnold cites as one of the fruitful men of English Puritanism and Nonconformity trained within the pale of the Establishment.

memes.label: John William Colenso
memes.description: British mathematician, theologian, and first Bishop of Natal (1814–1883), known for controversial biblical criticism; Arnold invokes him in Culture and Anarchy as a byword for intellectual revolution in received ideas.

memes.label: John Worth Edmonds
memes.description: American judge and prominent spiritualist (1799–1874), whom Arnold cites as an example of the lack of intellectual standards in American religious culture.

memes.label: Joseph Butler
memes.description: Joseph Butler (1692–1752), English bishop and moral philosopher, whose Analogy of Religion and Fifteen Sermons made him one of the most important Anglican thinkers — Arnold invokes him once, but at a structurally decisive moment, as proof that the Established Church produces thinkers of a moral and intellectual stature that Nonconformist communities cannot match.

memes.label: Joseph Joubert
memes.description: French moralist and essayist whose posthumously published Pensées deeply influenced Matthew Arnold's conception of culture and the Hellenic pursuit of inward perfection.

memes.label: King James Bible
memes.description: The Authorized Version of the Bible (1611), the foundational English translation that Matthew Arnold quotes throughout Culture and Anarchy as a shared cultural reference point transcending sectarian divisions.

memes.label: Licensed Victuallers
memes.description: A trade association representing publicans and licensed premises, cited by Arnold as an example of a Philistine interest group whose model of education is driven by commercial and sectarian concerns rather than by the pursuit of culture.

memes.label: London
memes.description: The capital city where Arnold's essay was written and where the Hyde Park Riots occurred — the center of English political, commercial, and cultural life, and the stage on which the drama of culture versus anarchy plays out

memes.label: Machinery
memes.description: Arnold's term for the external means — wealth, industry, population, freedom, railways, coal — that are falsely worshipped as ends in themselves, distracting from the true goal of human perfection

memes.label: Marcus Aurelius
memes.description: Marcus Aurelius (121–180 CE), Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher, author of the Meditations — Arnold invokes him in the Preface to Culture and Anarchy as an emblem of sincerity and inwardness, a pagan who "lived and worked" his philosophy with a seriousness that shames the merely professed Christianity of the modern Philistine.

memes.label: Martin Luther
memes.description: German Protestant reformer whom Arnold characterises as a Philistine of genius, representing the moral energy of Hebraism without the balance of Hellenism.

memes.label: Matthew Arnold
memes.description: Matthew Arnold (1822–1888), Victorian poet, critic, and social thinker — author of Culture and Anarchy and the central consciousness through which the book's arguments are filtered

memes.label: Montesquieu
aliases: Baron de La Brede et de Montesquieu

memes.label: Most Cited Pages
memes.description: Top 50 most-cited entry pages across all types in the Culture and Anarchy wiki, ranked by total cross-reference count.

memes.label: Nonconformists
memes.description: The Protestant dissenters from the Church of England — Arnold's living example of Hebraism unchecked by Hellenism, whose moral seriousness he respects but whose cultural narrowness he critiques as an obstacle to national perfection

memes.label: Nonconformists (Organization)
memes.description: The collective body of Protestant dissenters from the Church of England — a powerful religious and political force that Arnold treats as the chief carrier of Hebraism in contemporary English life

memes.label: Nonconformity
memes.description: The tradition of English Protestant dissent from the established Church of England, which Matthew Arnold critiques in Culture and Anarchy for its narrow Hebraism and resistance to the full development of human culture.

memes.label: Old Testament
memes.description: The first division of the Christian Bible, which Matthew Arnold characterises as the source of Hebraic moral discipline — a tradition centred on the consciousness of sin and the imperative of righteous conduct — and distinguishes from the New Testament's inward transformation.

memes.label: Core Oppositions in Culture and Anarchy
Organization Index
memes.description: Complete index of all organization pages in the Culture and Anarchy wiki, including churches, political bodies, newspapers, and societies that appear in Arnold's argument.

memes.label: Oscar Browning
memes.description: Eton assistant-master and contributor to the Quarterly Review who defended Eton against Arnold's criticisms of English public-school practices.

memes.label: Oxford
memes.description: The ancient university that Arnold holds up as an exemplar of the transformative power of 'sweetness and light' — a place whose beauty formed minds capable of flexibility amid industrial ugliness

memes.label: Oxford University
memes.description: The ancient English university where Matthew Arnold was educated and which serves in his argument as an institutional symbol of beauty, sweetness, and resistance to the utilitarian spirit of the age.

memes.label: Parliament
memes.description: The British legislature, which Arnold views as dominated by middle-class Philistine interests and incapable of embodying the nation's 'best self'

memes.label: Perfection
memes.description: The ultimate aim of culture in Arnold's framework — the harmonious and general development of all sides of human nature, both individual and social

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person pages in the Culture and Anarchy wiki, sorted alphabetically with quality tier and nationality.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: An index of all place pages in the Culture and Anarchy wiki, dynamically rendered from the page registry.

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: The ancient Greek philosopher (c. 428–348 BCE) whom Arnold repeatedly invokes as the classical fountainhead of the Hellenic spirit — the thinker who first articulated the ideal of seeing 'the true, firm, intelligible law of things'

memes.label: Thomas Henry Huxley
memes.description: English biologist and agnostic, mentioned in Culture and Anarchy as a target of religious critics who demanded that science provide moral remedies.

memes.label: Puritanism
memes.description: The historical religious movement and enduring cultural disposition that Arnold identifies as the chief vehicle of Hebraism in England — morally serious but culturally narrow, the primary target of his call for Hellenic supplement

memes.label: The Quarterly Review
memes.description: A conservative quarterly periodical that Arnold engages with in Culture and Anarchy as a voice of establishment opinion, particularly in its defense of Eton and the classical curriculum against his criticism of aristocratic education.

memes.label: Queen Elizabeth I
memes.description: Tudor monarch of England, referenced by Arnold as an example of the spirit of a great epoch contrasted with Victorian sectarian narrowness.

memes.label: Real Estate Intestacy Bill
memes.description: A proposed 1860s parliamentary bill to equalize inheritance among children, criticized by Arnold as based on a "mechanical maxim" rather than true reason.

memes.label: Reason and the Will of God
memes.description: Arnold's supreme standard, borrowed from Bishop Wilson — the fusion of rational understanding and divine purpose that culture seeks to make prevail in human affairs

memes.label: Reform Bill 1867
memes.description: The Representation of the People Act 1867, which extended the franchise to urban working-class men — the political event that forms the immediate backdrop to Arnold's essay and his anxieties about democracy without culture

memes.label: The Reform Crisis (1866–1867)
memes.description: The political crisis surrounding the passage of the Second Reform Act (1867), marked by mass agitation led by the Reform League, the Hyde Park Riots of July 1866, and heated parliamentary debate — Arnold's primary contemporary exhibit of the conflict between authority and 'doing as one likes'

memes.label: Reform League
memes.description: A working-class political organization founded in 1865 to campaign for universal manhood suffrage, frequently cited by Arnold as an example of Populace-driven political action and the operation of class interests in reform.

memes.label: Richard Baxter
memes.description: Seventeenth-century English Puritan divine, theologian, and writer, referenced by Matthew Arnold as a prime example of the fruitful men produced by the Established Church, and later criticized as one who "swims with the stream" of popular opinion.

memes.label: Robert Buchanan
memes.description: Scottish poet and critic whose verse about the suffering of urban children Arnold quotes at length in the Conclusion as an instance of popular poetry that mistakes sentiment for insight and "the bathos" for profundity.

memes.label: Ancient Rome
memes.description: The classical civilization that Arnold discusses alongside Greece as a source of Western culture, characterized by its organizational and administrative genius but ultimately, in Arnold's view, "mechanical and external" in its cultural achievement compared with the Hellenic ideal.

memes.label: St. Paul
memes.description: The apostle Paul (c. 5–67 CE), whom Arnold cites as the supreme example of Hebraism — the spirit that sets conduct and obedience above intellectual cultivation

memes.label: Samuel Morley
memes.description: English businessman, philanthropist and Liberal politician, mentioned in Culture and Anarchy in connection with education policy debates.

memes.label: Spontaneity
memes.description: The characteristic virtue of Hellenism — the free, flexible, undogmatic activity of the mind that sees things freshly without the constraint of rigid systems or habitual preconceptions

memes.label: Sweetness and Light
memes.description: Arnold's central metaphor for the harmonious perfection of human nature — the union of beauty (sweetness) and intelligence (light) that culture pursues

memes.label: The Reformation
memes.description: The sixteenth-century religious revolution that Arnold identifies as the great reassertion of Hebraism in Western Christianity — a necessary but ultimately one-sided development that 'stamped out' the Hellenic virtues of balance and flexibility

memes.label: The State
aliases: organ of our collective best self

memes.label: The Times
memes.description: The leading British newspaper of the Victorian era, which Arnold treats as the quintessential voice of middle-class Philistinism — self-satisfied, doctrinaire, and hostile to the free play of mind that culture requires

memes.label: Thomas Bazley
memes.description: Manchester industrialist and Liberal MP whose parliamentary speeches on free trade and manufacturing interests Arnold presents as the quintessential voice of middle-class Philistinism — earnest, prosperous, and indifferent to "sweetness and light."

memes.label: Thomas Carlyle
memes.description: Thomas Carlyle (1795–1881), Scottish essayist and historian, a powerful influence on Arnold's generation whose Hebraic moral fervor Arnold both admires and critiques as an example of Hebraism unchecked by Hellenism

memes.label: William Chambers
memes.description: British Member of Parliament and rigid Hebraiser, introduced the Deceased Wife's Sister Bill in the House of Commons.

memes.label: William Edward Baxter
memes.description: British Liberal politician and philosophical "swimmer with the stream" whom Arnold critiques as representative of superficial political thinking.

memes.label: William Murphy
memes.description: Anti-Catholic lecturer active in Birmingham whose inflammatory Protestant rhetoric Arnold presents as a specimen of the "ordinary self" operating in the religious sphere, paired with Charles Bradlaugh as a mirror image of undisciplined political passion.

memes.label: Dr. William Smith
memes.description: Nineteenth-century English lexicographer, editor of the Quarterly Review, and compiler of widely-used school-books, referenced by Matthew Arnold in a satirical passage about the puffing of one's own publications.

memes.label: Zephaniah Diggs
memes.description: Arnold's self-described "poor old poaching friend," cited in Culture and Anarchy as a humorous illustration of the working-class defect in sympathy and action
